name: Mazda
bloodline_detail: High Tottori (Kedaka) genetics; one of very few Tottori-line foundation blacks available outside Japan.
marbling_note: Reputed among the best-marbling of the 1976 import bulls; Mazda-descended cattle have topped modern bull-test marbling despite the Tottori line's growth/frame reputation.
bio: Mazda was one of only four Wagyu bulls, and one of two Japanese Black bulls, brought to the United States in the historic first export from Japan, arranged in 1976 by individual importer Morris Whitney (not the 'Morris family'). Sourced from Tottori Prefecture, he carried the larger-framed Kedaka/Tottori bloodline, cattle originally bred as pack animals in the grain trade and prized for strong backlines and growth. With no fullblood Wagyu females yet in America, Mazda was joined to Angus, Holstein, Hereford and Brangus cows; Colorado State University collected his semen for research before the bulls passed to Wagyu Breeders Inc. Whitney's venture underwhelmed commercially and the four bulls were sold to Texas veterinarian Dennis Wendt of Georgetown a few years later. A quirk of the record: the import paperwork reportedly described the bulls as Friesians. Mazda's genetics endured through his marbling reputation, with breeders reporting Mazda-bred carcasses out-marbling cattle stacked with elite Tajima lines. Because his semen is rare it commands premium prices at auction. He remains a genetic touchstone for breeders seeking an authentic Tottori outcross that boosts both marbling and frame.
photo_note: Period photograph of Mazda from the collection of Washington-state Wagyu breeder Mr. Reeves, scanned for WagyuTank — believed to be the first authenticated image of the bull published online.
notes: Registration FB103/IMUFA0103, Tottori origin, 1976 Whitney import and ~94 AU progeny corroborated across Rocking 711, Y2 Wagyu and Wagyu International. Birth year and sire/dam not published in accessible sources; left null/empty.

name: Mt. Fuji
bloodline_detail: Hyogo Prefecture origin with Tajima (Tajiri) line breeding, the compact high-marbling strain underpinning Kobe beef.
marbling_note: Marbling considered average for the breed; transmits lighter birth weights, easy calving, smaller mature cow size, above-average milk, smaller ribeye and relatively low retail yield; valued for maternal and calving-ease traits.
bio: Mt. Fuji was the second of the two Japanese Black bulls in the landmark 1976 importation by Morris Whitney, the first Wagyu ever exported from Japan to the United States. Drawn from Hyogo Prefecture, he carried Tajima-line genetics, the same compact, intensely marbling strain behind Kobe beef, making him a cornerstone of foundation Black breeding in North America. Like his herdmates he arrived to a country with no fullblood Wagyu females, so he was first crossed onto British and dairy breeds while Colorado State University collected and studied his semen; the four bulls were later bought by Wagyu Breeders Inc. and eventually sold to Texas veterinarian Dennis Wendt. Marketed today as 'The Original,' Mt. Fuji semen remains scarce and sought after. His breeding profile leans maternal: he stamps lighter birth weights, easy calving, strong milk and moderate, refined females rather than heavy carcass yield, with marbling that grades average for the breed. For breeders chasing authentic early-import Tajima blood and calving ease, Mt. Fuji sits about as far back as any North American pedigree can reach.
photo_note: Period photograph of Mt. Fuji from the collection of Washington-state Wagyu breeder Mr. Reeves, scanned for WagyuTank — believed to be the first authenticated image of the bull published online.
notes: Registration FB104/IMUFA0104, Hyogo/Tajima origin, 1976 import and ~74 AU progeny confirmed across nuwagyu, Rocking 711 and Wagyu International. Birth year and sire/dam not published; left null/empty.

name: Rueshaw
bloodline_detail: Kumamoto Red (Akaushi) line from the heart of Japan's Akaushi region; 1975 Japanese Red National Champion.
marbling_note: Foundation marbling powerhouse: his line-bred descendant JC Rueshaw 75 posted the highest-ever marbling scan and largest ribeye at the 2014 Houston Livestock Show; that bull with his semen sold for a Wagyu-world-record $500,000 in 2022.
bio: Rueshaw is the most storied of America's Akaushi founders, the 1975 Japanese Red National Champion and, by repute, the only national-champion Wagyu ever permitted to leave Japan. Born 2 February 1973 in Kumamoto Prefecture, the ancestral home of the Akaushi breed, he was one of the two red bulls in Morris Whitney's four-bull 1976 importation, the first Wagyu shipment from Japan to the United States, and he holds the distinction of being the very first Wagyu registered with the American Wagyu Association. With no fullblood females present, Rueshaw was initially bred to domestic cows while Colorado State collected his semen; the bulls later passed to Wagyu Breeders Inc. His influence on the American Akaushi population is profound and still commercially electric: a line-bred descendant, JC Rueshaw 75, recorded the largest ribeye and highest marbling scan in Houston Livestock Show history in 2014, and in 2022 that bull with his semen inventory sold for a Wagyu world-record $500,000. Sired by Ruemei H39 out of FB2 Kikutama 1285, Rueshaw combined championship phenotype with elite marbling, and his scarce semen still commands premium prices as breeders chase authentic foundation Akaushi genetics.
photo_note: Primary image from the Akaushi Genetics foundation-sires archive. A rare alternate period photograph — from the wall of Washington-state Wagyu breeder Mr. Reeves — is shown in the gallery below.
notes: Identity, 1975 championship, 1976 Whitney import, first AWA registration and 2 Feb 1973 birth corroborated across Akaushi Insight, Akaushi Genetics and Wagyu International. Registration AF6808 and pedigree (Ruemei H39 x FB2 Kikutama 1285) primarily from Akaushi Genetics; AU progeny not published.

name: Judo
bloodline_detail: Kumamoto Red (Akaushi) line; valued as a genetic outcross within the narrow Akaushi foundation base.
marbling_note: Prized primarily as a rare genetic outcross within the narrow Akaushi foundation rather than for one carcass trait; F11 carrier with very limited semen availability.
bio: Judo, correctly the fourth bull of the 1976 importation (never 'Mikimoto'), was the second Akaushi bull, alongside Rueshaw, brought to the United States by individual importer Morris Whitney in the first-ever Wagyu export from Japan. Sourced from Kumamoto Prefecture, the cradle of the Akaushi breed, he arrived to a country with no fullblood females and was used, like the others, for graded-up research breeding while Colorado State collected his semen; the bulls were subsequently bought by Wagyu Breeders Inc. Because only four founder bulls and, later, a small band of Akaushi females ever left Japan, Judo's chief value to modern breeders is as a genetic outcross: his blood widens an unusually narrow Akaushi foundation and helps manage inbreeding in a closed population. Recorded as sired by Shigetakara H 40 out of Sakae RK23331, he is noted as an F11 carrier, and his semen is exceptionally scarce, making him a sought-after but hard-to-source outcross sire. Though less celebrated than the champion Rueshaw, Judo remains one of the irreplaceable pillars of American Akaushi, and correcting the record on his identity matters: the 1976 red pair was Rueshaw and Judo, both from Kumamoto.
photo_note: Period photograph of Judo from the collection of Washington-state Wagyu breeder Mr. Reeves, scanned for WagyuTank.
notes: 1976 Whitney import and Kumamoto Red origin well corroborated (Rocking 711, Wagyu International, Akaushi Insight). Registration EAF97014 and pedigree (Shigetakara H 40 x Sakae RK23331) are single-source (Akaushi Genetics); birth year and AU progeny not published, left null.

name: Shigemaru
bloodline_detail: Kumamoto Red (Akaushi); paternal brother to the renowned cow-maker Namimaru. Part of the 1994 joint-venture import.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (Englewood Farm / HeartBrand Beef)
marbling_note: Regarded as the most versatile foundation Akaushi sire for adding dimension and balance; F11 carrier used to broaden carcass and phenotype quality across the American Akaushi herd.
bio: Shigemaru was one of the three Akaushi bulls in the pivotal 1994 importation, the second and final export of Akaushi from Japan, that established HeartBrand Beef's herd. The cattle were selected in Kumamoto Prefecture and purchased by Dr. Al and Marie Wood of Englewood Farm, Texas, then shipped in the Japanese-American joint venture alongside a band of elite red females; this Akaushi foundation came through the Woods and HeartBrand, not Takeda (a common error). A paternal brother to the celebrated cow-maker Namimaru, Shigemaru is prized by breeders as the most versatile of the founder sires, the bull used to make Akaushi cattle 'more dimensional,' improving balance, structure and overall carcass quality rather than a single trait. Sired by Dai-Ni 2 Shigenami Tokkyu 27 out of Dai-Yon 4 Akibare Tokkyu 3357, he is recorded as an F11 carrier. His son Samsung had one of the longest tenures as a herd bull in HeartBrand history, noted for feet and leg design, fertility and progeny quality. Because only three bulls and a small band of females founded the entire American Akaushi population, Shigemaru's genetics are foundational and heavily leveraged, and he remains a benchmark sire for well-rounded Kumamoto Akaushi blood.
photo_note: Photo hosted on Akaushi Genetics foundation-sires page; verified HTTP 200, image/jpeg.
notes: 1994 Wood/HeartBrand import and Kumamoto origin corroborated across HeartBrand, Akaushi Insight and Wagyu International. Registration AF110 and pedigree single-source (Akaushi Genetics); birth year and progeny counts not published.

name: Tamamaru
bloodline_detail: Kumamoto Red (Akaushi) line from the 1994 joint-venture import; noted for structural size and stoutness.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (Englewood Farm / HeartBrand Beef)
marbling_note: Renowned for consistency and structural excellence; breeders say no other foundation sire adds comparable length of body and stoutness; used to build frame and mass. Noted F11-free.
bio: Tamamaru was among the three Akaushi bulls imported in 1994, the second and final Akaushi export from Japan that seeded HeartBrand Beef's foundation herd. Selected in Kumamoto Prefecture and purchased by Dr. Al and Marie Wood of Englewood Farm, Texas, he came to America through the Japanese-American joint-venture shipment alongside a small group of elite red females and the bulls Shigemaru and Hikari, an Akaushi line that traces to the Woods and HeartBrand rather than to Takeda. Within the tiny founder population, Tamamaru fills a distinct and valuable niche: he is the sire breeders reach for to add frame, described as unmatched for the 'length of body and stoutness' he transmits, along with a reputation for consistency and structural excellence. Recorded as sired by Dai-Go 5 Tamanami Tokkyu 96 out of Dai-Hachi 8 Marunami, he is noted as F11-free, an advantage for breeders managing that recessive. Because just three bulls founded the entire U.S. Akaushi herd, Tamamaru's blood is woven throughout modern pedigrees registered with the American Wagyu Association and the Akaushi Association of America. For programs needing size, length and skeletal soundness without sacrificing Akaushi carcass quality, Tamamaru remains an indispensable foundation sire.
photo_note: Photo hosted on Akaushi Genetics foundation-sires page; verified HTTP 200, image/jpeg.
notes: 1994 Wood/HeartBrand import and Kumamoto origin corroborated across HeartBrand, Akaushi Insight and Wagyu International. Registration AF109 and pedigree single-source (Akaushi Genetics); birth year and progeny counts not published.

name: Hikari
bloodline_detail: Kumamoto Red (Akaushi) line from the 1994 import; sire line traces to Dai 10 Mitsumaru, one of Japan's most elite Akaushi bulls.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (Englewood Farm / HeartBrand Beef)
marbling_note: Maternal-and-calving specialist: sons noted for calving ease and daughters for superior milk production, making him a foundation source of fertility and easy-fleshing females; F11 carrier.
bio: Hikari was the third of the three Akaushi bulls in the 1994 importation, the second and final Akaushi export from Japan that founded HeartBrand Beef's herd. The animals were chosen in Kumamoto Prefecture and purchased by Dr. Al and Marie Wood of Englewood Farm, Texas, arriving in the Japanese-American joint-venture shipment together with the bulls Shigemaru and Tamamaru and a band of elite red females, confirming that the American Akaushi foundation came via the Woods and HeartBrand, not Takeda. Hikari's contribution is decidedly maternal: breeders value him because his sons pass on calving ease while his daughters excel in milk production, making him a foundation source of the fertility, udder quality and easy-fleshing females that anchor commercial Akaushi programs. His sire line is especially prestigious, tracing to Dai-Ju 10 Mitsumaru KO 76, counted among Japan's most elite Akaushi bulls, out of the dam Kazutomo 1KYU 92958; he is recorded as an F11 carrier. With only three bulls seeding the entire U.S. Akaushi population, Hikari's genetics run deep through pedigrees registered with both the American Wagyu Association and the Akaushi Association of America. For breeders prioritizing maternal strength, milk and trouble-free calving, Hikari is the founder sire of choice.
photo_note: Photo hosted on Akaushi Genetics foundation-sires page; verified HTTP 200, image/jpeg.
notes: 1994 Wood/HeartBrand import and Kumamoto origin corroborated across HeartBrand, Akaushi Insight and Wagyu International. Registration AF107 and pedigree single-source (Akaushi Genetics); birth year and progeny counts not published.

name: Michifuku
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima (Yasumi-Doi family)
marbling_note: Regarded by many as the best overall carcass bull ever exported from Japan; former #1 marbling bull in the U.S. Wagyu Sire Summary, consistently high in ribeye area.
bio: Michifuku (FB1615) is one of the most influential marbling sires in Wagyu history and a cornerstone of the Tajima line that underpins Kobe beef. He was flown from Japan in July 1993 by the Mannett Group (which became World K's / Westholme) among the first Black Wagyu exported to North America, landing initially in Canada with the Wagyu Sekai program alongside Haruki 2 and a group of Tajima-influenced heifers. A 100% Tajima bull of the Yasumi-Doi family, he was sired by the celebrated Monjiro 11550 out of Michiko J655635. Michifuku is widely described as the best overall carcass bull ever to leave Japan, and he topped the U.S. Wagyu Sire Summary for marbling while ranking near the top for ribeye area. His genetics spread explosively through both American and Australian herds, with roughly 5,600 registered progeny in Australia alone, and he is frequently credited with keeping the early Fullblood population viable. Offspring are noted for thick, well-muscled builds, outstanding marbling and calm dispositions. His birth year is not authoritatively documented in accessible sources.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms Michifuku FB1615 profile photo (file is named fb1516.jpg on their server, likely a filename typo); verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Registration FB1615 / WKSFM0164 confirmed across Rocking 711 and Prime Valley. Birth year left null per instruction, no authoritative source found. AU progeny 5,597 per Wagyu International foundation page (Rocking 711 lists a slightly lower 2019 snapshot ~4,927).

name: Haruki 2
bloodline_detail: 56% Tajima, 19% Shimane (Fujiyoshi), 13% Itozakura, 6% Kedaka
marbling_note: One of the most consistent Wagyu marbling bulls and a balanced sire adding growth and milk; also one of only three foundation sires with a positive grass-fed score.
bio: World K's Haruki 2 (FB1614) is a prominent foundation Fullblood sire and a half-brother to Michifuku, both sired by Monjiro 11550. His dam is Sakura 2 J741638. Haruki 2 was among the very first Black Wagyu bulls exported from Japan, flown out in July 1993 by the Mannett Group (later World K's / Westholme) alongside Michifuku and three Tajima-influenced heifers; the first Fullbloods born outside Japan were second-generation embryos sired by Haruki 2. Unlike his purely Tajima half-brother, Haruki 2 is a genetically balanced bull, roughly 56% Tajima with meaningful Shimane (Fujiyoshi), Itozakura and Kedaka contributions, which made him valued for well-rounded outcomes rather than marbling alone. Breeders prize him for small-birth-weight calves that grow rapidly to above-average weaning and yearling weights, for daughters with superior milk, and for excellent temperament, and he is one of only three foundation sires with a positive grass-fed score. He remains one of the most consistent marbling sires used across North America and Australia, with roughly 1,665 registered progeny in Australia. His birth year is not authoritatively documented.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms Haruki 2 FB1614 profile photo; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Registration FB1614 / WKSFM0139 and bloodline percentages confirmed by Rocking 711 and Prime Valley. Classified Mixed given the 56/19/13/6 split. AU progeny 1,665 per Wagyu International foundation page. Birth year null, not found.

name: Kenhanafuji
bloodline_detail: 75% Fujiyoshi / 25% Kedaka (no Tajima blood)
marbling_note: Renowned maternal sire respected for producing superior Fullblood females: large frames, high productivity, feed efficiency and good temperament. Sire Itohana was among Dai 7 Itozakura's highest-marbling sons.
bio: Kenhanafuji (FB2461) is the rarest of the original Japanese foundation bulls and one of the most valuable Wagyu sires ever, nicknamed the 'International Bull of Mystery' and cited as the highest-selling Wagyu bull of all time. He is genetically distinct from the famous Tajima marbling sires: he carries no Tajima blood, instead being roughly 75% Fujiyoshi (Shimane) and 25% Kedaka. His pedigree runs Itohana x Kensei x Itochiyo; his sire Itohana (FB504) was a linebred Dai 7 Itozakura son and one of that line's highest-marbling sires in Japan, and his dam was Hana Fuji 472029 (FB563). Kenhanafuji was imported to North America in 1994 by the Mannett Group (later World K's), in the same shipment as Takazakura. He is celebrated above all as a maternal sire, producing large-framed, productive, feed-efficient, good-tempered daughters, and only a handful of North American ranches ever held semen on him, keeping straw prices high and rising. He recorded roughly 403 registered progeny in Australia. His birth year is not documented in the sources reviewed.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms Kenhanafuji FB2461 profile photo; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Registration FB2461 / IMUFN2461 and 75% Fujiyoshi / 25% Kedaka confirmed by Rocking 711, Prime Valley and Wagyu World. Importer per Wagyu International = Mannett Group 1994. AU progeny 403 per Wagyu International foundation page. Birth year null.

name: Fukutsuru 068
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima
marbling_note: Ranked #1 in marbling in every WSU / National Wagyu Sire Summary from 2001 to 2016; produces exceptionally fine marbling, best used as a terminal sire. F11 carrier.
bio: JVP Fukutsuru 068 (FB2101) is arguably the most famous Wagyu bull in American history and a benchmark for the Tajima marbling type. Born 29 May 1992 in Hyogo Prefecture, he is 100% Tajima, sired by Dai 2 Yasutsuru 774 out of Teruyasu J649663; his maternal granddam was the legendary 'Hyogo Cow,' Kikutsuru. He was exported to the United States in 1994 through Japanese Venture Partners (JVP). For a remarkable stretch he ranked #1 in marbling in every Washington State University / National Wagyu Sire Summary from 2001 through 2016, only displaced (by Sanjirou 3) to #2 in 2017. Fukutsuru is prized for producing exceptionally fine, dense marbling and is typically used as a terminal sire; his calves tend to be smaller-framed with slower growth but superb carcass quality, so breeders pair him for marbling rather than size. He is an F11 carrier and free of other known genetic conditions. As his straws became rare, semen prices climbed sharply. He recorded roughly 1,000 registered progeny in Australia and remains a defining name in the Tajima marbling tradition worldwide.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms JVP Fukutsuru 068 FB2101 profile photo; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Registration FB2101 / IMJFMJ068 confirmed by Rocking 711 and Prime Valley. Birth year 1992 (29 May 1992) applied per mandatory correction. Imported 1994 by JVP. AU progeny 1,000 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Takazakura
bloodline_detail: Tajima-influenced; sire Takaei J1412 is a son of Yasufuku J930 (Tajima), combined with several Takeda lineages. No clean published percentage.
marbling_note: Growth-and-carcass sire: in an Australian progeny test roughly 1,200 F1 calves fed 540 days averaged ~1.87 lb/day gain with 68% grading 9+ on the Australian scale. Adds frame and muscle with strong meat quality.
bio: WKS Takazakura (FB2892) was one of two Black Wagyu bulls imported in 1994 by the Mannett Group (later World K's / Westholme), in the same shipment as Kenhanafuji. He was sired by Takaei J1412 out of Dai Ni Sakura 7; Takaei was one of the best-known sons of the great Tajima sire Yasufuku J930, making Takazakura one of relatively few Yasufuku grandsons available outside Japan. His pedigree also draws on several noted Takeda lineages. Unlike the pure-marbling Tajima specialists, Takazakura is valued as a growth-and-carcass sire that adds frame, body length and muscle while retaining strong meat quality and calm temperament. In a large Australian progeny test roughly 1,200 F1 calves fed for 540 days averaged about 1.87 lb per day gain with around 68% grading 9 or higher on the Australian marble scale, a standout commercial result. He recorded roughly 355 registered progeny in Australia. His birth year is not documented in the sources reviewed.
photo_note: No verifiable direct image of Takazakura found; Prime Valley and Wagyu World bull pages returned 404 and probed image paths did not resolve. Empty rather than fabricate.
notes: Registration FB2892 / WKSFN2892, sire Takaei J1412, dam Dai Ni Sakura 7 confirmed by Rocking 711 / Wagyu International. Bloodline classified Mixed (Yasufuku/Tajima via Takaei plus Takeda lines, no clean percentage). Prefecture Hyogo inferred from Yasufuku/Takaei origin. AU progeny 355 per Wagyu International foundation page. Birth year null.

name: Kikuyasu-400
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima; linebred Kikunoridoi (Kikuyasu line)
marbling_note: The largest and heaviest 100% Tajima bull ever to leave Japan (~1,938-1,980 lb); used to add size and ribeye while retaining Tajima marbling. Consistently top-ranked for ribeye area.
bio: JVP Kikuyasu-400 (FB2100) is the flagship sire of the Kikuyasu line and one of the original Tajima bulls imported to the United States by Japanese Venture Partners in 1994. He is 100% Tajima and linebred on Kikunoridoi, arguably the most famous bull ever bred in Hyogo Prefecture, home of Kobe beef, with his maternal grandsire Kikuterudoi being a Kikunoridoi son and among the highest-marbling bulls in Wagyu history. His sire, Kikuyasu-Doi, produced the Grand Champion group at the Japanese National Carcass Competition. Kikuyasu-400's distinguishing trait is size: at roughly 1,938-1,980 lb he was the largest and heaviest pure-Tajima bull ever exported from Japan, and breeders use him to add frame and ribeye area to Tajima programs without sacrificing the line's signature fine marbling. He recorded roughly 611 registered progeny in Australia and remains a foundation sire behind many American and Australian herds, his semen becoming scarce and valuable over time. His exact birth year and dam are not documented in the sources reviewed.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms Kikuyasu-400 FB2100 profile photo; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Registration FB2100 / IMUFM2100, 100% Tajima linebred Kikunoridoi, and ~1,938-1,980 lb weight confirmed by Rocking 711, Prime Valley and Lone Mountain. Sire Kikuyasu-Doi; dam not found. AU progeny 611 per Wagyu International foundation page. Import year 1994 (JVP).

name: Yasutanisakura
bloodline_detail: Yasutani Doi line of Tajima (Hyogo); sire Yasukane 1682, maternal grandsire Dai 7 Itozakura, giving heavy Doi marbling plus unusual milking ability.
marbling_note: From the Yasutani Doi line, regarded by Japanese semen graders as the highest-quality marbling and best-tasting fat of all Tajima lines; carrier B3/CL16/F11, CHS free.
bio: Yasutanisakura, best known as JVP Yasutanisakura 931 (FB2102), is a Fullblood Black Tajima sire imported to the United States by Japanese Venture Partners in 1994 alongside the black bulls Kikuyasu and Fukutsuru. He descends from the revered Yasutani Doi line of Tajima out of Hyogo, a line Japanese national semen salesmen call the finest for marbling and fat flavour. His sire is Yasukane 1682 and his dam Nakatani 1851772, with Dai 7 Itozakura appearing as maternal grandsire. That Itozakura cross is credited with giving him a rare combination for a heavy-Doi bull: intense marbling plus genuine milk and maternal ability, making him a favourite for building breeding females. He recorded roughly 129 registrations in Australia. Genetically he tests carrier for B3, CL16 and F11 and free for CHS. His legacy sits at the luxury end of the Wagyu spectrum, prized where no-expense-spared marbling is the goal, and his blood flows into elite Doi-influenced marbling sires used worldwide.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms FB2102 profile photo; verified live as image/jpeg.
notes: Birth year not confirmed; left null. Import year 1994 (JVP) per Wagyu International. AU progeny 129 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Itomichi 1/2
bloodline_detail: Predominantly Shimane genetics (reported ~85% Shimane, ~13% Tajima, ~2% Kedaka); sired by Itomichi, grandsired by Dai 7 Itozakura crossed onto the Dai 2 Kintou cow family.
marbling_note: Used to increase mature size and growth rate without reducing marbling; a key vehicle for Shimane growth genetics outside Japan.
bio: Itomichi 1/2 (TF36, FB2126) is a single bull whose registered name literally reads 'Itomichi 1/2', not two separate animals. He was one of the five black bulls (with Kikuhana, Itohana 2, Kinto and Terutani) exported by Hokkaido-based breeder Shogo Takeda beginning in 1994, the shipments that first allowed Fullblood Wagyu breeding outside Japan and reached the U.S. alongside 35 black heifers. His genetics are predominantly Shimane, commonly cited near 85% Shimane, 13% Tajima and 2% Kedaka, carried through his sire Itomichi and grandsire Dai 7 Itozakura, Shimane's most celebrated bull, crossed onto the Dai 2 Kintou cow family. Breeders value him for adding mature frame, growth rate and structural size while preserving marbling, which made him a workhorse for producing larger-framed Fullbloods and strong maternal lines. He is one of the most prolific Takeda foundation sires, with roughly 1,136 registrations in Australia. His descendants, such as the widely catalogued MR Itomichi 1/2 5725, keep his growth-plus-marbling profile circulating in modern herds across Australia and North America.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms FB2126 profile photo; verified live as image/jpeg.
notes: 'Itomichi 1/2' is ONE bull (the '1/2' is part of the registered name), so a single object is returned. Birth year not confirmed (Takeda export 1994). Dam not confirmed. Bloodline percentages are as reported by breeder sources. AU progeny 1,136 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Itohana 2
bloodline_detail: Linebred Dai 7 Itozakura (Fujiyoshi/Shimane), which appears on both paternal and maternal strands; sired by Itohana J809 (son of Dai 7 Itozakura J65) out of Aino 6 J674297, matriarch of the modern Aizakura cow family.
marbling_note: Adds frame size, growth and strong milking ability to Tajima-based herds; progeny show the lightest average time on feed (~16.8 months) among the group while grading well.
bio: TF Itohana 2 (widely listed as TF38) is a Fullblood Black Fujiyoshi/Shimane bull born 22 October 1993 and exported to the United States by Takeda Farm as a two-year-old, part of Shogo Takeda's foundational consignments. He is intensely linebred to Dai 7 Itozakura, Shimane's most famous sire, which appears on both strands of his pedigree. His sire is his namesake Itohana J809 (a son of Dai 7 Itozakura J65) and his dam is Aino 6 J674297, matriarch of the modern Aizakura cow family. Breeders prize him as a frame- and growth-adding sire that lifts mature size and, notably, milking and maternal ability in Tajima-heavy herds, making him ideal for producing breeding females rather than terminal marbling. His progeny record the lightest average time on feed of the group (about 16.8 months) while still grading well. He is one of the most influential Takeda sires abroad, with roughly 3,014 registrations in Australia, and remains a cornerstone of maternal Itozakura/Fujiyoshi development outside Japan.
photo_note: Primary breeder page (Designer Wagyu) now redirects to a parked domain; no verified direct image of this specific bull was located, so left empty rather than risk a wrong image.
notes: AWA FB registration number not confirmed (left null); TF designation is TF38 (fits the Takeda TF36-39 sequence). Import to US ~1995 (born 1993). AU progeny 3,014 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Kinto
bloodline_detail: Shimane genetics tracing to Dai 7 Itozakura twice on the paternal side and once maternally; maternal line traces to the Ezokintou / Dai 2 Kintou (Haraharashu) cow family known for large, quality carcasses.
marbling_note: From the Dai 2 Kintou line noted for consistently large, quality beef; adds size and growth, though offspring are sometimes reported as high-strung in disposition.
bio: Kinto (TF39, FB2290) is one of the five black bulls Shogo Takeda exported beginning in 1994, the Takeda shipment (with Itomichi 1/2, Kikuhana, Itohana 2 and Terutani) that established Fullblood Wagyu outside Japan and first reached the U.S. in 1995 alongside 35 black heifers. His pedigree is heavily Shimane, tracing to Dai 7 Itozakura twice through the paternal line and once maternally, while his maternal line runs back to the Ezokintou line and the Dai 2 Kintou cow family of the Haraharashu strain, genetics long associated with consistently large, high-quality carcasses. Breeders use him to add mature size and growth to their herds, though his offspring are sometimes described as higher-strung in temperament, a trait managers weigh against his frame and carcass contribution. He is among the less numerous Takeda foundation sires, with roughly 147 registrations in Australia. Despite the smaller footprint, he remains a documented pillar of the Shimane/Kintou growth genetics that diversified the early Fullblood gene pool in North America and Australia.
photo_note: No verified direct image of this specific bull located (Prime Valley reg-based image path returned 404); empty rather than fabricate.
notes: Sire and dam not confirmed; birth year unknown (Takeda export ~1994, US arrival 1995). A second registration FB2299 also appears in one catalogue listing. AU progeny 147 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Kikuhana
bloodline_detail: Shimane/Kiku (Kikumon) line; sired by the famous Itohana, a linebred Dai 7 Itozakura, giving him strong Itozakura/Fujiyoshi foundation.
marbling_note: Known for larger, faster-growing offspring with excellent phenotype and daughters with good maternal traits, while retaining Shimane marbling quality.
bio: Kikuhana (TF37, FB2127) is a Fullblood Black Shimane sire from the Kiku (Kikumon) family, exported by Shogo Takeda among his first five black bulls beginning in 1994 and arriving in the U.S. in 1995. He was sired by the famous Itohana, a bull linebred to Dai 7 Itozakura, anchoring Kikuhana firmly in the Itozakura/Fujiyoshi foundation that Takeda drew from Shimane's Nougi and Nita districts. Breeders value him for producing larger, faster-growing calves with excellent phenotype and for throwing daughters with strong maternal and milking traits, making him a useful blend of growth and cow-family quality. His genetics were later used, for example, over Itomichi-sired foundation heifers in early Australian herds such as those assembled by Sumo Wagyu. He is a moderately prolific Takeda sire, with roughly 538 registrations in Australia. As one of the named pillars of Takeda's Shimane exports, Kikuhana helped broaden the maternal and growth base of Fullblood Wagyu populations across both Australia and North America.
photo_note: No verified direct image of this specific bull located; Prime Valley reg-based image path returned 404. Empty rather than fabricate.
notes: Dam and birth year not confirmed. Bloodline is Shimane/Kiku (Kikumon line). AU progeny 538 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Terutani
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima of the Nakadoi sub-strain, carrying Kikunori Doi genetics with a balanced combination of Kikumi Doi and Yasumi Doi blood. 'TF' = Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda). The low TF number reflects the earlier 1994 export cohort.
marbling_note: Meat-quality specialist driven more by ribeye size and retail yield than extreme marbling; low birthweights, slower growth, smaller mature size. Crossed onto Itomichi-rich females produced A5 carcasses ~70% of the time; progeny required the longest average time on feed (26.1 months) of the foundation sires.
bio: Terutani, tag TF40, is a Takeda Farm foundation bull and one of the earlier of Mr Shogo Takeda's black-Wagyu exports, shipped from Japan in 1994 in a group of five bulls (with Itomichi 1/2, Kikuhana, Itohana 2 and Kinto) alongside 35 black females. His lower 'TF40' number reflects that earlier registration. He is 100% Tajima of the Nakadoi sub-strain, carrying Kikunori Doi genetics with a balanced combination of Kikumi Doi and Yasumi Doi blood. Terutani is a specialist rather than an all-rounder: he throws low birthweights, slower growth and smaller mature size, but strong meat quality driven more by ribeye size and retail yield than by extreme marbling. Crossed onto Itomichi-rich females he produced A5 carcasses about 70% of the time, more than three times the Japanese average, though his progeny required the longest average time on feed (26.1 months) of the foundation sires. With roughly 580 Australian-registered progeny, he is one of the least numerous foundation bulls but a valued low-birthweight outcross.
photo_note: No direct verified image file located for this bull on the sources checked; none fabricated.
notes: Correctly TF40 (a common error mislabels Terutani as TF148, which is Itoshigenami). Sire/dam and birth year not found. 1994 Takeda export cohort. AU progeny 580 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Kitaguni Jr
bloodline_detail: Son of Kitaguni 7-8 J1530 (the famous Dai 7 Itozakura son whose dam was Kedaka) out of Nakayuki J13943; reported composition ~37% Tajima, 25% Itozakura, and ~12.5% each Kedaka/Shimane/Okayama, a genuine cross-line bull.
importer: World K's / bred from imported Kitaguni 7-8 genetics
marbling_note: Calves show increased frame and enhanced growth with beef grading Prime+ to Prime+++; blends Itozakura/Shimane marbling with Kedaka growth.
bio: Kitaguni Jr (FB2422), born 1994, is a Fullblood Black bull prized as a cross-line sire linking the Shimane/Fujiyoshi and Kedaka/Tottori worlds. He is the son of Kitaguni 7-8 J1530, the most famous son of Dai 7 Itozakura J65 whose own dam was a Kedaka cow, making Kitaguni 7-8 a celebrated Dai 7-Kedaka composite, out of Nakayuki J13943, so Kitaguni Jr is a grandson of Dai 7 Itozakura. Breeder analyses put his makeup near 37% Tajima, 25% Itozakura and about 12.5% each of Kedaka, Shimane and Okayama, a deliberately balanced mix. That combination gives his calves noticeably larger frame and enhanced growth while still grading Prime+ to Prime+++, uniting Itozakura marbling with Kedaka size. In large feeding trials his F1 progeny fed 540 days achieved about 1.8 lb ADG with 65% grading 9+ on the Australian scale. He recorded roughly 489 registrations in Australia. For breeders seeking to add growth and outcross diversity without abandoning marbling, Kitaguni Jr remains one of the go-to composite foundation sires.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms FB2422 profile photo; verified live as image/jpeg.
notes: Bloodline set to 'Mixed' (a genuine Dai 7/Itozakura x Kedaka composite); ties to the Kitaguni 7-8 Kedaka/Tottori line via his sire. Wagyu International indicates he was produced from imported females/genetics rather than imported as a live bull, so exact import mechanism and year are uncertain (import_year null). AU progeny 489.

name: Yasufuku Jr
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima; the only son of the legendary Yasufuku J930 available as a sire outside Japan, with dam sire Monjiro 11550 and Yasumi Doi J10328 appearing three times in his pedigree.
marbling_note: Elite marbling: ranked #1 for IMF% both as sire (33.34% IMF, 35 carcasses) and dam sire (34.11% IMF) in the 2017 LMCC Sire Performance Study; F1 trials showed ~78% grading 9+ on the Australian scale.
bio: Yasufuku Jr (FB4697), born 2 September 1997, is a 100% Tajima Fullblood Black bull and one of the most valuable marbling sires ever exported. He was brought out as a bull calf by the Mannett Group (Mannet Company) in the venture's final 1997 shipment, arriving with seven black heifers. He is the only son of the legendary Yasufuku J930 available as a sire outside Japan, with maternal grandsire Monjiro 11550 and the great Yasumi Doi J10328 appearing three times in his pedigree, a concentration of the finest Tajima marbling blood. His performance record is exceptional: in the 2017 LMCC Sire Performance Study he ranked #1 for intramuscular fat both as a sire (33.34% IMF) and as a dam sire (34.11% IMF), and in large trials roughly 1,200 F1 calves fed 540 days reached ~1.94 lb ADG with 78% grading 9+ on the Australian scale. He carries CL16. With about 908 registrations in Australia, Yasufuku Jr is a cornerstone marbling sire whose blood commands premium demand across the global Wagyu industry.
photo_note: No verified direct image of this specific bull located (Prime Valley reg-based image path returned 404); empty rather than risk a mismatched photo.
notes: Born 2 Sep 1997. One source gives his birthplace as Tottori Prefecture while his bloodline is 100% Tajima (Hyogo origin); prefecture set to Hyogo for the Tajima line with the Tottori birth claim noted. Dam not confirmed. AU progeny 908 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Hirashigetayasu
bloodline_detail: Predominantly Kedaka line through sire Dai 20 Hirashige (J287); carries Tajiri/Tajima via maternal grandsire Tayasu Doi (J7208). Name fuses 'Hirashige' + 'Tayasu'. SCD AA.
importer: Westholme (Chris Walker), via ET Japan Company, Hokkaido
marbling_note: Valued for growth, frame, milk and size rather than extreme marbling; carcasses grade on the high end. One of only three foundation sires (with Itomoritaka 002 and Haruki 2) carrying a positive grass-fed score.
bio: Hirashigetayasu, tag '001' (ETJ001, J2351, herdbook FB670), is one of three patriarch bulls imported to the USA in 1997 by Chris Walker of Westholme from the ET Japan Company in Hokkaido, alongside Itomoritaka '002' and Kitateruyasudoi '003'. His semen was then used heavily in Australia, founding a large branch of the national herd. Sired by the celebrated Kedaka-line bull Dai 20 Hirashige out of Dai 5 Yuruhime, with the Tajiri sire Tayasu Doi as maternal grandsire, his very name fuses 'Hirashige' and 'Tayasu'. Predominantly Kedaka, he is prized for contributing growth, frame, milk and size to outcross programs, and is one of only three foundation sires (with Itomoritaka and Haruki 2) with a positive grass-fed score, an asset for pasture systems. He tests SCD AA and free of known recessives. With roughly 3,246 Australian-registered progeny, he ranks among the most influential foundation sires in both Australia and the USA and remains a go-to source of frame and maternal size.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms sire page (fb670.jpg); verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Birth year not confirmed (likely early 1990s). Prefecture reflects Kedaka bloodline origin; animal was physically bred/collected via ET Japan in Hokkaido. AU progeny 3,246 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Itomoritaka
bloodline_detail: Fujiyoshi (Shimane) line; sired by Itohirashige (FB680) out of Dai 6 Oefujii (Kuro Koh, FB679). Identified in initial foundation testing as a carrier of a hereditary recessive condition.
importer: Westholme (Chris Walker), via ET Japan Company, Hokkaido
marbling_note: Maternal specialist, not a marbling bull: carries the highest milk EBV in the Australian BREEDPLAN (also high in the USA). Daughters become outstanding milking dams; progeny combine strong growth and ideal frame. Positive grass-fed score.
bio: Itomoritaka, tag '002' (ETJ002, J2703, herdbook FB681), nicknamed 'Rocky,' is the second of Westholme's three patriarch bulls, imported from ET Japan Company (Hokkaido) to the USA in 1997 by Chris Walker and then used extensively in Australia. He represents the Fujiyoshi (Shimane) line, sired by Itohirashige (FB680) out of Dai 6 Oefujii (Kuro Koh, FB679). His defining legacy is maternal: Itomoritaka carries the highest milk EBV in the Australian BREEDPLAN (and ranks high in the USA), so his daughters become outstanding milking dams while his progeny pair strong growth with ideal frame. Like Hirashigetayasu he carries a rare positive grass-fed score, making him valuable for pasture systems. Breeders use him to add size, milk and maternal strength rather than extreme marble score. Initial foundation testing identified him as a carrier of a hereditary recessive condition, so matings are managed accordingly. He had roughly 1,551 Australian-registered progeny and remains the premier maternal foundation sire of the Westholme group.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms sire page (fb681.jpg); verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Birth year not confirmed. Recessive-carrier status per Wagyu International recessives reference. AU progeny 1,551 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Kitateruyasudoi
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima; a linebred Kikuterudoi son of Terunaga Doi, tracing repeatedly to Yasumi Doi (J10328) and Yasutani Doi (J472). The marbling/carcass anchor of the Westholme import group.
importer: Westholme (Chris Walker), via Mr Daiji Imori of ET Japan Company, Hokkaido
marbling_note: Widely regarded as the best marbling and carcass-trait proven sire of the Westholme group; progeny fed in Japan reliably hit the A5 target. Fourth-best marbling sire in the U.S. and top 2% for birth weight. Heaviest average induction weights among foundation sires (326.3 kg from 51 progeny).
bio: Kitateruyasudoi, tag '003' (ETJ003, J2810, herdbook FB686), born 18 March 1993, is the marbling powerhouse of Westholme's three patriarch bulls, exported as a four-year-old to the USA in 1997 (via Mr Daiji Imori of ET Japan) and used heavily in Australia. He is 100% Tajima: a linebred Kikuterudoi son of Terunaga Doi (FB685) out of Yoshimi 3 (FB684), tracing repeatedly to Yasumi Doi and Yasutani Doi. Where '001' and '002' brought size and milk, '003' brought carcass quality; he is widely regarded as the best marbling and carcass-trait proven sire of the Westholme group, with progeny fed in Japan reliably hitting the A5 grade, and he ranks among the top marbling sires in the U.S. while also sitting in the top 2% for birth weight. He recorded the heaviest average induction weights among foundation sires (326.3 kg from 51 progeny). His influence runs through elite sons such as Macquarie C1176, Macquarie Y408 and Kitahatsuhi 97/1. With about 4,101 Australian-registered progeny, he is one of the most heavily used Tajima foundation sires in Australia.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms ETJ003 Kitateruyasu Doi (FB686) sire page; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Registration FB686, sire Terunaga Doi (FB685) and dam Yoshimi 3 (FB684) confirmed from Prime Valley Farms. Birth date 18 March 1993 per breeder sources. AU progeny 4,101 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Itoshigenami
bloodline_detail: Approximately 75% Tajima with some Shimane and Kedaka; sired by Shigeshigenami out of Fukuyuke. 'TF' = Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda). SCD VV; F11 carrier.
importer: Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda) to USA; Australian herd acquired by Dr Simon Coates (Sumo Wagyu)
marbling_note: Considered by the Australian Wagyu industry the #1 marbling foundation sire; progeny average ~8.6/9 on the Australian marbling scale. Snowflake-marbling Kumanami/Shigekanenami trait. The most-registered foundation sire in Australia.
bio: Itoshigenami, tag TF148 (FB3682), is arguably the single most important Tajima marbling sire ever to leave Japan. He was among the black Wagyu bulls bred by Mr Shogo Takeda of Takeda Farm and exported to the USA in the 1990s; the 'TF' prefix marks Takeda Farm animals. After US quarantine the Takeda herd dispersed to Australia and Canada, and Dr Simon Coates (Sumo Wagyu) acquired the Australian portion, making TF148 a cornerstone of the industry. Sired by Shigeshigenami (a double cross of Shigekanenami, deeply inbred in the Kumanami strain) out of Fukuyuke, he is roughly 75% Tajima with some Shimane and Kedaka, and his pedigree carries the famous snowflake marbling of the Shigekanenami/Kumanami family. His progeny average an exceptional 8.6 out of 9 on the Australian marbling scale, and the Australian Wagyu industry ranks him its #1 marbling foundation sire; producer David Blackmore rates him among the top three bulls ever exported from Japan. With about 6,220 Australian-registered progeny, the most of any foundation sire, his fine dense marbling underpins countless modern pedigrees and standout sons such as Mayura Itoshigenami Jnr and Sumo Itoshigenami C0158. He tests SCD VV and is an F11 carrier.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms TF148 Itoshigenami (FB3682) sire page; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Correctly TF148 (US herdbook FB3682), not Terutani. Birth year not confirmed. AU progeny 6,220 per Wagyu International foundation page (highest of any foundation sire).

name: Itoshigefuji
bloodline_detail: Just over half Shimane with Okayama and Kedaka; linebred to the legendary Shimane sire Dai 7 Itozakura. Sired by Itofuji out of Dai 30 Noboru (a daughter of Itomichi J1158, Takeda's famed Dai 7 Itozakura son). Tenderness score 7.
importer: Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda) to USA; Australian herd acquired by Dr Simon Coates (Sumo Wagyu)
marbling_note: Larger frame with beautiful marbling, averaging ~8.5/9 on the Australian scale, plus notably tender beef (tenderness 7). Daughters valued for milk and calm temperament. One of the four most-used foundation sires in Australia.
bio: Itoshigefuji, tag TF147 (FB3681), was born 18 September 1995 at Teruo Nakamura's farm and exported to the USA by Takeda Farm in the late 1990s, later anchoring Australian and Canadian herds. He is linebred to the legendary Shimane sire Dai 7 Itozakura: sired by Itofuji out of Dai 30 Noboru, a daughter of Itomichi J1158, Takeda's famous son of Dai 7 Itozakura and one of only a handful of Itozakura's 1,000-plus sons to outperform him for marbling and growth. Just over half Shimane, with Okayama and Kedaka blood, TF147 combines a larger frame with beautiful marbling, averaging about 8.5 out of 9 on the Australian scale and producing notably tender beef (tenderness score 7). His daughters are valued for excellent milk and calm temperament. Together with Itoshigenami TF148, Michifuku and Kitateruyasudoi '003', he is among the four most-used foundation sires in Australia, accounting with them for roughly half of all Australian registrations, and he recorded about 4,168 Australian-registered progeny.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms TF147 Itoshigefuji (FB3681) sire page; verified HTTP 200 image/jpeg.
notes: Correctly TF147 (US herdbook FB3681), not TF148. Bloodline tagged Itozakura for its dominant Dai 7 Itozakura linebreeding; equally describable as 'over half Shimane'. Born 18 Sep 1995. AU progeny 4,168 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Itozuru Doi
bloodline_detail: Approximately 50% Tajima, 25% Fujiyoshi, 25% Kedaka; descends from Itokitazuru J1081, a son of Dai 7 Itozakura (grandsire). Dam Yasuhime J433313 was by Yasumi Doi J10328 out of a Kikuteru Doi daughter (Naka Doi/Kikumi Doi lines). 'TF' = Takeda Farm.
importer: Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda) to USA; herd dispersed to Australia and Canada
marbling_note: Growth/carcass-oriented outcross rather than a pure marbling bull; among foundation-sire groups his progeny produced some of the heaviest average carcasses (~457 kg after 21.4 months on feed). Carries famous Itozakura growth genetics.
bio: Itozuru Doi, tag TF151, is a Takeda Farm ('TF') foundation bull exported to the USA in the 1990s and dispersed into the Australian and Canadian herds. Genetically he is a blend, approximately 50% Tajima, 25% Fujiyoshi and 25% Kedaka, descending from Itokitazuru J1081, one of the important sons of the great Shimane sire Dai 7 Itozakura (his grandsire). His dam, Yasuhime J433313, was sired by Yasumi Doi J10328 out of a Kikuteru Doi daughter, weaving Naka Doi/Kikumi Doi lines into his pedigree. That diversity makes him useful as an outcross that still carries famous Itozakura growth genetics into marbling-heavy herds. His progeny are known for size and carcass weight; among foundation-sire groups his produced some of the heaviest average carcasses (about 457 kg after 21.4 months on feed). With roughly 2,632 Australian-registered progeny, he is a solidly used, growth-oriented foundation sire, though less numerous than the top Tajima marbling bulls.
photo_note: No direct verified image file located for this bull on the sources checked; none fabricated.
notes: Sire name not confirmed (descends from Itokitazuru J1081 line). Birth year and prefecture not found. Bloodline set to Mixed given the explicit 50/25/25 composition. AU progeny 2,632 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Kikuterushige
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima; a linebred Kikuterudoi bull and one of only two sons of the great Japanese sire Tanifuku Doi available outside Japan. 'TF' = Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda).
importer: Takeda Farm (Mr Shogo Takeda) to USA; herd dispersed to Australia and Canada
marbling_note: Described by Mr Shogo Takeda as the highest-marbling bull he raised in his lifetime. Trade-offs: poor temperament in some progeny, slow growth and inconsistent ('shotgun') results; used selectively, often via embryo transfer, for peak marbling.
bio: Kikuterushige, tag TF150, is one of Takeda Farm's black Wagyu foundation bulls exported to the USA in the 1990s, after which the Takeda herd was dispersed to Australia and Canada. He is 100% Tajima, a linebred Kikuterudoi bull and one of only two sons of the great Japanese sire Tanifuku Doi available outside Japan (the other being Kikutsuru Doi TF146). Mr Shogo Takeda regarded him as the highest-marbling bull he raised in his lifetime, a remarkable statement from Japan's most exacting marbling breeder. That extreme marbling potential comes with trade-offs frequently noted by breeders: poor temperament in some progeny, slow growth and inconsistent ('shotgun') results, so he is used selectively, often through embryo transfer, to inject peak marbling rather than as a broad-use sire. His concentrated Tajima genetics make him prized for terminal marbling crosses. With roughly 1,142 Australian-registered progeny, he is less numerous than the headline foundation sires but genetically significant for herds chasing maximum marble score.
photo_note: No direct verified image file located for this bull on the sources checked; none fabricated.
notes: Dam not found; sire Tanifuku Doi per breeder descriptions. Birth year not found. AU progeny 1,142 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Kikutsuru Doi
bloodline_detail: Pure Tajima Kiku line; sired by Tanifuku Doi (a top Tajima Doi sire) out of the famous cow Kikutsuru J978542, the 'Hyogo Cow', one of the most important cows of the Tajiri line.
importer: Takeda Farm (Shogo Takeda) to USA; herd dispersed to Australia and Canada
marbling_note: Small-framed (~1,300 lb) with outstanding marbling and Doi-quality fat; bred over Kinu 1 line females yields steers with extreme marbling and prepotent bulls.
bio: Kikutsuru Doi (TF146, FB3615) is a Fullblood Black Tajima sire and one of the most marbling-focused of Takeda's exports. He is the first of two sons of the top Tajima Doi sire Tanifuku Doi to become available outside Japan (his brother is Kikuterushige TF150), and his dam is Kikutsuru J978542, the legendary 'Hyogo Cow,' considered one of the most important cows of the Tajiri line and named for Hyogo, home prefecture of genuine Kobe beef. This pairing places him in the pure Tajima Kiku family renowned for the finest, best-tasting fat, the same maternal line behind Fukutsuru 068. He is a smaller-framed bull at just over 1,300 pounds, so breeders typically mate him to larger Wagyu females to lift their marbling while managing frame; used over Kinu 1 line cows he produces steers with extreme marbling and bulls with strong genetic potency. His semen is comparatively rare and his Australian progeny count is modest (about 40). Carrying the celebrated Doi and Hyogo-Cow blood, Kikutsuru Doi is a specialist marbling sire whose descendants anchor the high end of Tajima quality outside Japan.
photo_note: Prime Valley Farms FB3615 profile photo; verified live as image/jpeg.
notes: TF146 / FB3615. Birth year and import year not firmly confirmed (Takeda cohort, US late 1990s). Sire/dam confirmed across breeder sources (Tanifuku Doi x Kikutsuru 'Hyogo Cow'). AU progeny 40 per Wagyu International foundation page.

name: Mitsuhikokura
bloodline_detail: Reported as one of the highest-percentage Shimane bloodline bulls exported by Takeda; Fujiyoshi (Okayama/Shimane) line carrying Fujiyoshi sire Dai 14 Shigeru plus Tanshige and Kumanami-strain Tajima marbling genetics.
marbling_note: Blends Tanshige and Kumanami-strain Tajima marbling with Fujiyoshi maternal genetics; noted for growth, milk production and thick build, and for producing outstanding breeding females.
bio: Mitsuhikokura (registered by some sources as TF149, FB3683) is a Fullblood Black bull from Shogo Takeda's exports and is described as one of the highest-percentage Shimane bloodline sires Takeda sent abroad. He sits in the Fujiyoshi group, the Okayama/Shimane maternal line, and is reported to carry the Fujiyoshi sire Dai 14 Shigeru along with marbling genetics from the Tanshige and Kumanami strains of Tajima, a blend aimed at combining carcass quality with strong maternal performance. Breeders favour him for growth, milk production and a thick, well-muscled build, and especially for producing outstanding breeding females with exceptional milking ability, which is why he is used to build cow herds rather than purely as a terminal marbling sire. His pedigree is cited in connection with elite lines such as Yasufuku J930 and World K's Takazakura. As a high-Shimane outcross to the more common Tajima-heavy genetics abroad, Mitsuhikokura helped diversify and strengthen the maternal base of Fullblood Wagyu herds in North America and Australia.
photo_note: No verified direct image of this specific bull located; Prime Valley reg-based image path returned 404. Empty rather than fabricate.
notes: TF number ambiguous: some breeder sources call Mitsuhikokura TF149, but Wagyu International's foundation page lists TF149 as 'Kitatsurukikura' (AU progeny ~97) as a distinct entry, so these may be the same animal under variant romanization or two different bulls. Registration FB3683 and the TF149 alias are therefore uncertain; sire, dam, birth year, import year and AU progeny not confirmed and left null. Bloodline Fujiyoshi/Shimane per breeder descriptions.

name: Kamui
bloodline_detail: Not a standard Wagyu bloodline: Kamui is a purebred Mishima, the rare native cattle of Mishima Island (Yamaguchi Prefecture). The island herd was rebuilt after Rinderpest and inbred for 25+ generations; Mishima are ancestral to modern Wagyu but are NOT classified as Wagyu in Japan.
marbling_note: Mishima cattle show high marbling in a much smaller-framed animal than modern Wagyu; used to introduce rare native genetics rather than for commercial carcass size.
bio: Kamui (registration B115, also known as TF152, 'Kamui Kamui' or 'Wakausa') is essentially the only Mishima bull ever exported from Japan and a genuine rarity in the cattle world. Born 28 August 1991, he is not a Wagyu but a purebred Mishima, the native cattle of tiny Mishima Island off Yamaguchi Prefecture. That island herd was devastated by disease and rebuilt from a handful of mainland Japanese Black cattle, then closed and inbred for more than 25 generations, leaving Mishima as a living link to the ancestral stock from which today's Wagyu descend. Because Mishima are not considered Wagyu in Japan, Kamui is registered by the American Wagyu Association as a base animal, and his calves out of registered Fullblood Wagyu dams enter the herdbook as percentage animals (50% Wagyu on the first cross). Mishima are prized for high marbling in a notably smaller frame than modern Wagyu. In Australia, breeders such as Moondarra bred up entire Mishima-influenced lines from this genetics. Kamui stands as a singular conservation and curiosity sire, carrying irreplaceable native Japanese-island genetics into breeding programs abroad.
photo_note: No verified direct image of Kamui located; empty rather than fabricate.
notes: Breed = Mishima and birth year 1991 (28 Aug 1991) confirmed per correction and Wagyu International. Bloodline enum set to 'Mixed' because Mishima is not one of the standard Wagyu bloodlines; explained in bloodline_detail. Prefecture Yamaguchi (Mishima Island). Sire/dam and import year not confirmed. TF152 designation and 'Kamui Kamui/Wakausa' aliases per Moondarra.

name: Itotani
bloodline_detail: Early Itozakura/Fujiyoshi-influenced black bull; the 'Ito' prefix marks the Dai 7 Itozakura family. Exact line composition not authoritatively published; treated as Fujiyoshi/Itozakura pending confirmation.
marbling_note: Genetic value was as an outcross that widened an extremely narrow early gene pool rather than for a single documented carcass trait; carcass data not published in accessible sources.
bio: Itotani is the forgotten early founder: chronological import histories describe him as 'the fifth bull,' whose semen was brought to Canada in about 1991 by Lakeside Industries of Brooks, Alberta, two years before the famous 1993 Michifuku/Haruki shipment. At that point only the four 1976 Whitney bulls existed outside Japan, so Itotani's genetics were prized specifically for widening a dangerously narrow foundation gene base in North America. He was a Japanese Black of the Itozakura/Fujiyoshi world, and his semen entered early Canadian and, by extension, North American percentage-breeding programs before fullblood females were available. Because he predates the well-documented 1993-1997 waves and was never a live import, he is routinely omitted from foundation-sire rosters (including the current WagyuTank list), yet Wagyu International and Rocking 711's chronological histories explicitly name him as the first genetics to arrive after 1976. Detailed pedigree, prefecture and progeny records are sparse, reflecting how early and how limited the import was.
notes: Named explicitly in Rocking 711 and Wagyu International chronological histories as the 1991 Lakeside/Canada semen import ('fifth bull'). Registration, sire/dam, birth year and bloodline percentages NOT published — left null/low. Confidence low on genetic detail, high on the import event.

name: Shigefuku
bloodline_detail: Main Kedaka line; a son of Dai 20 Hirashige (himself a son of Kedaka), inheriting strongly from Eiko. Growth/frame-type Kedaka genetics rather than marbling-type Tajima.
importer: Westholme (Chris Walker) via ET Japan Company, Hokkaido
marbling_note: A growth-and-frame sire, not a marbling specialist: noted for higher growth rates, greater body volume and desirable coat type; contributes size and constitution to the Kedaka side of the herd.
bio: Shigefuku, tagged '005' in the Westholme numbering that continues from the live '001'/'002'/'003' patriarch bulls, is one of three additional black sires whose SEMEN Chris Walker's Westholme program imported from the ET Japan Company (Hokkaido) in 1998, arriving alongside 59 more females the year after the 1997 live shipment. He is a son of Dai 20 Hirashige, himself a son of Kedaka, placing Shigefuku deep in the main Kedaka (Tottori) lines and making him a paternal relative of Hirashigetayasu '001'. Breeders value him for the classic Kedaka contribution: higher growth rates, greater body volume, strong constitution and desirable coat type, with a noted strong inheritance from the influential bull Eiko. Because he entered only as semen and only via Australia (some of which was re-exported to the USA), he is easily missed on live-import foundation lists, but he is a genuine exported foundation sire and part of the Westholme genetic package that built much of the Australian and American Kedaka growth base.
notes: Sire Dai 20 Hirashige and Kedaka/Eiko heritage confirmed by Reserve Cattle Company importation history and Wagyu International. Semen imported ~1998 (year after the 1997 Westholme live trio). Dam, birth year and registration not published; left null. Confidence high on identity/line, low on pedigree specifics.

name: Dai 6 Seizan
bloodline_detail: An Itozakura/Tajima cross carrying Tottori/Kedaka growth and maternal traits plus Itozakura (Okayama/Fujiyoshi) size, with Kikunori Doi marbling introduced through Kanehagi. A deliberately composite growth-plus-marbling sire.
importer: Westholme (Chris Walker) via ET Japan Company, Hokkaido
marbling_note: Balances size and marbling: growth and maternal strength from the Tottori/Kedaka side, frame/size from Itozakura, and enhanced marbling contributed through Kikunori Doi via the bull Kanehagi. Positioned as a growth-with-quality composite rather than a pure marbling bull.
bio: Dai 6 Seizan, tagged '006' and sometimes called simply 'the 6th Westholme bull,' is the second of the three black sires whose semen Westholme imported from ET Japan (Hokkaido) in 1998 with a consignment of 59 females. Genetically he is one of the more interesting composites among the foundation sires: an Itozakura/Tajima cross that layers Tottori/Kedaka growth and maternal ability, Itozakura (with roots in Okayama and Fujiyoshi) for frame and size, and Kikunori Doi marbling introduced through the bull Kanehagi. That blend made him a valuable tool for breeders wanting to add growth and body volume without abandoning marbling potential, complementing the pure-Tajima marbling of '003' Kitateruyasudoi and the Kedaka growth of '005' Shigefuku within the same import group. Like the other Westholme semen sires he never physically left Japan as a live animal and travelled via Australia, which is why he is routinely absent from live-import foundation rosters despite being a genuine exported foundation sire.
notes: Itozakura/Tajima composite with Kedaka growth and Kikunori-Doi-via-Kanehagi marbling per Reserve Cattle Company / Wagyu International histories. Semen imported ~1998. Sire, dam, birth year, registration not published; left null. Bloodline set 'Mixed' given the explicit composite. Confidence medium on line description, low on pedigree specifics.

name: Kitatsurukiku Doi
bloodline_detail: Tajima Doi line; a son of Dai 2 Yasutsuru Doi (the same sire as JVP Fukutsuru 068) and a direct descendant of the legendary 'Hyogo Cow' Kikutsuru. Concentrated Kikutsuru/Yasutsuru Doi marbling genetics.
importer: Westholme (Chris Walker) via ET Japan Company, Hokkaido
marbling_note: Marbling-oriented Tajima Doi sire: shares the elite Dai 2 Yasutsuru Doi sire with Fukutsuru 068 and traces to the Hyogo Cow Kikutsuru, the same maternal fountainhead behind the breed's finest fat; used to concentrate high-end Tajima marbling.
bio: Kitatsurukiku Doi, tagged '007,' is the third and most marbling-focused of the three black sires whose semen Westholme imported from ET Japan (Hokkaido) in 1998. His pedigree is a headline in itself: he is a son of Dai 2 Yasutsuru Doi, the very bull that sired JVP Fukutsuru 068, arguably the most famous marbling sire in American Wagyu history, and he descends directly from the legendary 'Hyogo Cow' Kikutsuru, the Tajiri-line matriarch behind the breed's finest, best-tasting fat. That places Kitatsurukiku Doi firmly in the pure Tajima Doi marbling world and made his semen a way to fold Fukutsuru-adjacent genetics into herds through an independent line. Because he arrived only as semen, routed through Australia, he is consistently omitted from live-import foundation lists, and his romanized name is easily confused with the Takeda-linked 'Kitatsurukikura' (a separate animal); the current WagyuTank data already flags that name collision under Mitsuhikokura. He is a genuine exported foundation sire and a valuable, under-recognized Tajima marbling source.
notes: Sire Dai 2 Yasutsuru Doi (shared with Fukutsuru 068) and descent from Hyogo Cow Kikutsuru confirmed by Reserve Cattle Company importation history and Wagyu International. Semen imported ~1998. NAME-COLLISION CAUTION: distinct from the Takeda 'Kitatsurukikura (TF149?)' referenced in the repo's Mitsuhikokura notes. Dam, birth year, registration not published. Confidence medium.

name: World K's Sanjirou
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima, linebred to the legendary Yasumi Doi J10328 (FB548). Sired by World K's Michifuku (FB1615) out of the elite imported cow Suzutani (FB1617).
importer: US-BORN (Hawkeye Breeders, Adel, Iowa) — not imported; born from imported parents
marbling_note: Elite carcass: ranked #3 in marbling in the 2006 and 2017 U.S. National Wagyu Sire Summaries — ahead of his own sire Michifuku — and top 1% for Ribeye Area EBV (~+9.1) and Retail Beef Yield (~+3.6). Used heavily to breed high-marbling, high-yield females.
bio: World K's Sanjirou (FB2501) is one of the most influential Wagyu bulls ever bred outside Japan, but he is a US-BORN second-generation sire, not a Japan export: he was born 28 June 1994 at Hawkeye Breeders in Adel, Iowa, from two imported parents. He is 100% Tajima and linebred to the legendary Yasumi Doi J10328, sired by World K's Michifuku out of Suzutani, the cow many regard as the best carcass female ever exported from Japan. Sanjirou repeatedly out-ranked his famous sire: he placed third for marbling in the U.S. National Wagyu Sire Summaries while also delivering exceptional ribeye area and retail yield, a rare marbling-plus-yield combination. Virtually every major American and Australian breeder used him to build female lines, and he founded a dynasty of influential sons. He belongs in the WagyuTank roster on the same logic the list already applies to Kitaguni Jr — a foundation-era sire produced from imported genetics — but he should be labeled clearly as US-born rather than a Japanese import.
notes: Born 28 Jun 1994, Hawkeye Breeders, Adel, Iowa; Michifuku x Suzutani, 100% Tajima linebred Yasumi Doi J10328. Registration FB2501 per Prime Valley Farms. NOT a Japan export — include only if the roster intends to cover 2nd-gen foundation sires (as it does for Kitaguni Jr). Confidence high.

name: World K's Shigeshigetani
bloodline_detail: 75% Tajima. Sired by World K's Haruki 2 (FB1614) out of the elite imported cow Suzutani (FB1617); carries the Kumanami-strain snowflake marbling of his maternal grandsire Shigeshigenami J10632.
importer: US-BORN (World K's program) — not imported; born from imported parents
marbling_note: Renowned as a prepotent marbling transmitter: World K's reported 3,800 F1 (50% Wagyu) cattle fed 540 days averaged ~2.156 lb/day ADG with ~90% grading 9+ on the Australian scale; consistently strong for eye-muscle area, retail yield and marble score.
bio: World K's Shigeshigetani (FB2907 / WKSFP1593) is, alongside Sanjirou, one of the two most famous 'second-generation' Wagyu sires produced outside Japan. He is US-born from imported parents: sired by World K's Haruki 2 out of Suzutani, the same elite Japanese cow that produced Sanjirou, making the two paternal-different maternal brothers. Roughly 75% Tajima, he carries the Kumanami-strain 'snowflake' marbling of his maternal grandsire Shigeshigenami J10632, one of Japan's most important historical sires (ranked #4 by Kenichi Ono behind Yasufuku J930, Dai 7 Itozakura and Kitaguni 7/8). Breeders prize Shigeshigetani for exceptional prepotency, the reliability with which he stamps outstanding, consistent marbling on his calves, backed by standout large-scale feeding data. Like Sanjirou, he is a foundation-era pillar of Australian and North American genetics but should be recorded as a US-born 2nd-generation sire, not a Japanese import — the same category the WagyuTank list already admits via Kitaguni Jr.
notes: Haruki 2 x Suzutani, 75% Tajima, maternal grandsire Shigeshigenami J10632; FB2907/WKSFP1593 per Rocking 711 and Prime Valley Farms. NOT a Japan export. Confidence high on identity/pedigree; birth year not published.

name: World K's Beijirou
bloodline_detail: Sired by World K's Haruki 2 (FB1614, ~56% Tajima/Fujiyoshi/Itozakura/Kedaka blend) out of the foundation cow Okutani; full brother to Genjiro. Inherits Haruki 2's balanced, non-pure-Tajima composition.
importer: US-BORN (World K's) — not imported; among the first fullblood calves born in the USA
marbling_note: Valued as a balanced growth-and-maternal sire in the Haruki 2 mould rather than an extreme-marbling specialist; specific carcass EBVs less documented than Sanjirou/Shigeshigetani.
bio: World K's Beijirou (FB2289) holds a place in Wagyu history as one of the very first fullblood Wagyu calves born in the United States: he and his sister Fujiko were born on 24 June 1994 out of the foundation cow Okutani, sired by World K's Haruki 2. He is US-born from imported parents, not a Japan export, and his registration number (FB2289) sits in the same early sequence as the Takeda live-import bulls, which is exactly why he is easy to mistake for an imported foundation sire. Genetically he follows his sire Haruki 2's balanced, non-pure-Tajima makeup, blending Tajima with Fujiyoshi, Itozakura and Kedaka, giving him a growth-and-maternal profile rather than the extreme marbling of the Michifuku-line 2nd-gen bulls. He is a full brother to Genjiro. Beijirou is worth including for historical completeness as a landmark first-generation American-born sire, clearly flagged as US-born rather than exported from Japan.
notes: First fullblood calves born in USA (Beijirou + sister Fujiko), 24 Jun 1994, Haruki 2 x Okutani; FB2289 per Prime Valley Farms. NOT a Japan export. Confidence medium (some sources distinguish 'the original Beijirou' from a same-named son).

name: Genjiro
bloodline_detail: Full brother to World K's Beijirou: sired by World K's Haruki 2 (FB1614) out of the foundation cow Okutani. Inherits Haruki 2's balanced Tajima/Fujiyoshi/Itozakura/Kedaka composition.
importer: US-BORN (World K's) — not imported; born from imported parents
marbling_note: Balanced growth-and-maternal Haruki-2-line sire; carcass documentation limited relative to the marquee 2nd-gen bulls.
bio: Genjiro (FB5200) is a US-born second-generation Wagyu sire and a full brother to World K's Beijirou, both sired by World K's Haruki 2 out of the important foundation cow Okutani. As a Haruki-2 son he carries the balanced, non-pure-Tajima composition that makes that line valued for growth, milk and maternal strength rather than extreme marbling alone. He is a minor but legitimate foundation-era sire that appears on breeder sire catalogs (e.g., Prime Valley Farms) and is included here mainly for completeness alongside his better-known brother; like the other World K's home-bred bulls he should be recorded as US-born from imported parents, not a Japanese import. Detailed pedigree beyond the sire/dam and carcass records are sparse in accessible sources.
notes: Full brother to Beijirou (Haruki 2 x Okutani); FB5200 per Prime Valley Farms. NOT a Japan export. Lowest priority of the additions. Confidence low-medium.
